title: Divided clock domain crossing at 2 GHz
description:
# Divided clock skew problem

A high-speed datapath runs at 2 GHz (500 ps period). A generated clock `clk_div2` runs at half-rate off the main clock. The worst setup violation is -85 ps on `stage1_reg/Q -> stage2_reg/D`, where `stage1_reg` is on `clk` and `stage2_reg` is on `clk_div2`.

Both clocks come from the same root, but `clk_div2` has an extra divider flop in its generation path. Uncertainty 30 ps.

**Your job:** figure out whether this is a data path problem or a clock network problem.

reportedViolation.summary: setup VIOLATED (-0.085 ns) on stage1_reg/Q -> stage2_reg/D (clk -> clk_div2)

rootCauseOptions.label: Insertion delay of clk_div2 lags clk by ~238 ps (div_flop CK→Q + buffer + wires), so the capture edge at stage2_reg arrives too late relative to launch
The XOR/AND chain in the datapath is too slow
stage1_reg drive strength is too low

acceptedFixes.description: Add matching buffers on the main clk branch to stage1_reg to equalize insertion delays across domains
Replace CKBUFX4 with CKBUFX8 and shorten the clk_div2 net to reduce insertion delay
Apply `set_clock_latency -source 0.1 [get_clocks clk_div2]` to model the generated-clock insertion delay explicitly and give the tool budget

hintTree.nudge: When the violating path crosses between two related clocks, the first question isn't 'is the data path too slow' — it's something about the clocks themselves.
hintTree.hint: Look at the clock network for both `clk` and `clk_div2`. Pay attention to how clk_div2 is generated and how many stages sit between the root clock and stage2_reg/CK.
hintTree.walkthrough: `report_clock_network -clock clk` shows stage1_reg gets clk at ~0.095 ns. `report_clock_network -clock clk_div2` shows stage2_reg gets it at ~0.333 ns (clk → div_flop CK→Q 0.16 + net 0.03 + CKBUFX4 0.048 + net 0.025 + the original clk latency 0.08). That 238 ps of extra insertion delay eats the budget. Fix: rebalance the main-clk branch to equalize, or set a source latency so STA knows about the generated-clock delay.

steps.title: Read the timing report
steps.goal: Understand the cross-domain path. Which clock launches and which captures?

options.label: Launch on clk (500 ps period), capture on clk_div2 (1 ns period)
Launch on clk_div2, capture on clk

steps.insight: This is a cross-domain path. clk launches at 2 GHz (500 ps) and clk_div2 captures at 1 GHz. The capture edge has a full 1 ns period, so you'd think there's plenty of margin. But is there?

steps.title: Check the data path
steps.goal: Is the combinational logic between the flops actually too slow, or is the problem elsewhere?

checkpoint.question: What's the total data path delay (CK→Q + cells + nets)?

options.label: ~381 ps — well under the 500 ps clk period

steps.insight: The data path is only ~381 ps — that should fit comfortably in even the 500 ps fast-clock period. The data path isn't the problem.

steps.title: Investigate both clock networks
steps.goal: Compare the insertion delay of clk at stage1_reg vs clk_div2 at stage2_reg. How is clk_div2 generated?

checkpoint.question: Why is clk_div2 arriving so late at stage2_reg?

options.label: clk_div2 passes through div_flop (CK→Q = 160 ps) + ck_buf_div + wires, adding ~238 ps of extra insertion delay vs clk
The clock buffer is too far away physically

steps.insight: clk_div2 is generated by div_flop, which adds its full CK→Q delay (160 ps) plus buffer/wire delay to the clock path. stage1_reg sees clk at ~95 ps; stage2_reg sees clk_div2 at ~333 ps. That 238 ps difference directly eats into the capture window.

steps.title: Determine the fix
steps.goal: The problem is insertion delay imbalance between two related clocks. What's the right fix strategy?

options.label: Rebalance: add buffers on the clk branch to stage1_reg so both domains have similar insertion delay
Resize the XOR and AND gates to be faster

steps.insight: When two related clocks have different insertion delays, the fix is clock tree balancing — not data path optimization. You equalize the launch and capture insertion delays so the clock relationship matches what STA expects.
